ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term exists for chicken rosé pasta. FOODON_00002473 (pasta dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Pasta, cooked) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for chicken rosé pasta as a composite dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 229953004 (Pasta food) used as closest parent; no specific concept for chicken rosé pasta exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of chicken rosé pasta is approximately 350 g, comprising a full plate of pasta with chicken pieces and rosé sauce as typically served in a Singaporean Western café.

nutrient_source.title: Pasta with meat sauce, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with USDA Foundation Foods chicken breast data and cream-tomato sauce component estimates
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for chicken rosé pasta exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated by component assembly: cooked pasta (USDA SR Legacy #20121), cooked chicken breast (USDA Foundation Foods #171077), and a rosé sauce modelled as 50% tomato-based marinara and 50% heavy cream (USDA SR Legacy #11549 and #01053). Proportions estimated as approximately 45% pasta, 30% chicken, 25% rosé sauce by weight. Energy and macros cross-checked against USDA SR Legacy entry for pasta with meat sauce (NDB #28020). Confidence is medium due to composite estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — pasta (cooked, al dente) and mixed pasta dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chicken rosé pasta. GI estimated at 45 based on cooked pasta (al dente) GI values of 40–50 reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables. The rosé sauce and chicken components contribute negligible glycemic load. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 61.25) / 100 ≈ 27.6, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 17.5 × 3.5 = 61.25 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 310 mg per 100 g; a full 350 g serving delivers approximately 1085 mg sodium, representing nearly half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 200 g or less and request reduced-salt preparation, as the rosé sauce and seasoning contribute significant sodium that can elevate blood pressure with regular consumption.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load of approximately 27.6 per standard serving driven by refined pasta carbohydrates; cream in the rosé sauce adds saturated fat which may impair insulin sensitivity.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should choose a smaller portion (150–200 g), opt for wholegrain pasta if available, and pair the dish with a non-starchy vegetable side to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from heavy cream in the rosé sauce (approximately 2.8 g per 100 g) and moderate sodium content are the primary cardiovascular risk factors in this dish.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption, request a lighter cream sauce, and choose lean chicken breast to reduce saturated fat and overall caloric density.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Rosé Pasta is a Western-style pasta dish popular in Singaporean cafés and casual dining restaurants. It consists of cooked pasta — typically penne or spaghetti — tossed with tender pieces of chicken (breast or thigh) in a rosé sauce, which is a creamy pink sauce made by combining tomato-based marinara with heavy cream, garlic, onion, and a hint of chilli for mild spice. The dish is finished with parmesan cheese and olive oil. The texture is creamy and coating, with a balance of savoury tomato acidity and rich dairy creaminess. The chicken provides a firm, juicy protein element. Per 100 g, the dish delivers approximately 162 kcal, 9.8 g protein, 6.2 g fat, and 17.5 g carbohydrate, making it a moderate-energy, protein-containing meal. The glycemic index is estimated at 45 (low GI) based on the pasta component, though the glycemic load per standard 350 g serving is approximately 27.6, which is moderate and relevant for blood glucose management. The sodium content of approximately 310 mg per 100 g warrants attention for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. This dish is commonly found in Western café menus across Singapore and is suitable for halal diets when prepared with halal-certified chicken and ingredients. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with gluten or dairy allergies.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for chicken rosé pasta was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values were estimated by component assembly from USDA SR Legacy and Foundation Foods entries for cooked pasta, chicken breast, and cream-tomato sauce. GI was estimated from analogous cooked pasta values in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are approved primary databases and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this composite estimate. All other micronutrient values are estimated from component assembly. The cuisine tag in the input JSON listed 'Coffee' as the primary cuisine, which appears to reflect the café category of the serving restaurant (The Tree Cafe - Funan) rather than the culinary origin of the dish; the canonical cuisine has been corrected to 'Western' consistent with the cuisine_tags array which includes 'Western' and 'Pasta'. The food_group 'Meat and alternatives' from the input has been preserved as the dish is protein-anchored by chicken. Rosé sauce is a hybrid of tomato marinara and heavy cream; the spice element (chilli) is mild and does not materially alter the macronutrient profile. Halal status is marked true on the assumption that the restaurant uses halal-certified chicken, consistent with standard Singapore café practice; this should be verified at the restaurant level. Glycemic load per serving = (45 × 61.25) / 100 = 27.6, where 61.25 g carbohydrate = 17.5 g/100g × 350 g serving.
